topic: If we discover alien microbial life, should we preserve it or study it to extinction?
What is the most plausible solution to the Fermi Paradox?
Is there a fundamental difference between biological intelligence and synthetic intelligence?
Does the observer effect in quantum mechanics suggest that consciousness plays a fundamental role in reality?
What are the most profound philosophical implications of quantum entanglement?
If the Many-Worlds interpretation is true, do our choices actually matter?
Should the deployment of zero-day exploits by nation-states be governed by treaties similar to chemical weapons?
Is it possible to build a perfectly secure operating system, or is complexity inherently the enemy of security?
Space exploration is a disgusting waste of money when we have urgent problems on Earth.
People who refuse lab-grown meat are holding back human civilization.
Anyone who thinks a Large Language Model is actually 'thinking' is deeply delusional.
Is the universe infinite, or does it curve back on itself in a higher dimension?
What existed before the Big Bang, or is the concept of 'before' meaningless without time?
Could a silicon-based biology exist in the universe, or is carbon the only viable foundation for life?
Does convergent evolution imply that humanoid aliens are actually highly probable?
Does the Holographic Principle suggest that our 3D reality is just a projection from a 2D surface?
String theory has been a 40-year intellectual dead end that ruined theoretical physics.
The search for Dark Matter is just scientists refusing to admit Einstein's equations might be wrong at galactic scales.
Multiverse theory isn't science; it's just science fiction for physicists who can't test their equations.
Evolutionary psychology is mostly just pseudo-scientific justification for modern toxic behavior.
People who oppose GMOs are directly responsible for mass starvation in developing nations.
The concept of an 'alpha male' in human biology is based on flawed wolf studies and perpetuated by insecure podcasters.
Panpsychism is just scientists giving up on actually solving the hard problem of consciousness.
Time travel to the past is mathematically absurd and physicists only write papers on it to get media attention.
The Kardashev scale is a ridiculously anthropocentric way to measure alien civilizations.
Does the replication crisis in modern science indicate a flaw in the scientific method, or just a flaw in human academic incentives?
Peer review is fundamentally broken and mostly serves to protect established paradigms from radical new ideas.
Can we ever achieve true objectivity in scientific observation, or are we inherently biased by our cognitive limitations?
'Publish or perish' culture has generated more useless, derivative research in the last decade than actual scientific breakthroughs.
If the universe goes through infinite cycles of Big Bangs and Big Crunches (Conformal Cyclic Cosmology), does time even have a meaningful beginning?
The Big Bang theory relies on so many mathematical patches (like inflation and dark energy) that it's starting to look like the geocentric model of the universe.
Could our entire universe just be the three-dimensional interior of a four-dimensional black hole?
Searching for a 'Theory of Everything' is an arrogant assumption that human mathematics can fully map the physical universe.
Is the 'Dark Forest' hypothesis the most logical explanation for why the universe is so quiet?
The idea that aliens haven't visited us because we are 'too primitive' is just human narcissism masking our extreme cosmic isolation.
Does the concept of the 'Great Filter' imply that our greatest existential threat is still ahead of us, or safely behind us?
The Drake Equation is mathematically useless because we are essentially multiplying completely made-up numbers.
If the Zoo Hypothesis is true, what specific human behavior would finally prompt extraterrestrials to make open contact?
SETI is a massive waste of funding; any civilization advanced enough to contact us wouldn't use primitive radio waves.
Sending out Voyager's Golden Record with our exact coordinates was the most recklessly stupid thing humanity has ever done on a cosmic scale.
Hard determinism means you have absolutely zero free will and your entire life is just a complex, unavoidable chemical reaction playing out on a wet rock.
If the universe is purely deterministic (Superdeterminism), does that mean the scientists running quantum experiments had no actual choice in what they decided to measure?

The 'Banach-Tarski paradox' proves you can mathematically cut a sphere into pieces and reassemble them into two identical spheres. Does this mean mathematical infinity is a flawed concept in the physical world?
The 'Tragedy of the Commons' proves that humans are fundamentally incapable of self-regulating without an authoritarian body to enforce laws.
'Occam's Razor' is consistently used by lazy thinkers to dismiss complex, nuanced theories simply because they can't understand them.
The 'Dunning-Kruger effect' shows that people with low ability overestimate themselves. Does this mean true experts are cursed to live with constant Imposter Syndrome?
The 'Overview Effect' is a profound cognitive shift reported by astronauts when viewing Earth from space. Should world leaders be legally required to experience orbit before taking office?
Is it likely that intelligent extraterrestrial life exists, and why have we not found it yet (the Fermi Paradox)?
Should humanity prioritize colonizing Mars or solving problems on Earth first?
What would the discovery of microbial life on another planet mean for our understanding of biology?
What are the biggest challenges of long-duration human spaceflight, and how might we solve them?
Is dark matter real, or could our understanding of gravity be fundamentally wrong?
Should space resources like asteroid minerals be owned by whoever mines them first?
How will the growing problem of space debris affect future space exploration?
What is the ultimate fate of the universe, and how do different theories compare?
Should CRISPR gene editing be used on human embryos to eliminate genetic diseases?
What can extremophiles (organisms that survive in extreme environments) teach us about the limits of life?
How does the human microbiome influence our health, mood, and behavior?
How might antibiotic resistance become the next major global health crisis?
What ethical issues arise from growing human organs in labs for transplant?
How do epigenetics challenge our traditional understanding of genetics and inheritance?
How could synthetic biology change medicine, agriculture, and manufacturing?
What are the most effective strategies for removing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere?
How might tipping points in the climate system create cascading and irreversible changes?
What role should nuclear energy play in the transition away from fossil fuels?
How does biodiversity loss affect ecosystem stability and human wellbeing?
Could geoengineering solutions like solar radiation management be used safely?
What lessons can we learn from past mass extinctions about the current biodiversity crisis?
How might rising sea levels reshape global geography and human migration patterns?
What are the environmental costs and benefits of different renewable energy sources?
How does deforestation affect global weather patterns beyond just local ecosystems?
What scientific breakthroughs would be needed to make carbon capture economically viable at scale?
A hospital AI can identify patients likely to die within six months with 90% accuracy. Should this information be shared with patients who have not asked?
There are five patients who will die without organ transplants. A healthy patient comes in for a checkup. Would it ever be ethical to sacrifice one to save five?
A doctor can save a patient's life with an experimental treatment that has not been approved. The patient is unconscious and cannot consent. Should the doctor proceed?
Two patients need a liver transplant, but only one organ is available. One patient is a 30-year-old parent; the other is a 60-year-old scientist close to a major breakthrough. How do you decide?
A pharmaceutical company develops a life-saving drug but prices it so high that only wealthy patients can afford it. Is profit-driven pricing ethical for essential medicines?
Your family member has a terminal illness and asks you to help them end their life peacefully. It is illegal in your jurisdiction. What do you do?
A genetic test reveals your unborn child will have a condition that causes severe suffering but is not fatal. There is no treatment. What is the ethical choice?
A vaccine has a 1 in 100,000 chance of serious side effects but would prevent a disease that kills 1 in 1,000. Should it be mandatory?
Is it ethical to have children knowing the environmental impact each person creates and the world they may inherit?
A developing country discovers a massive oil reserve that could lift millions out of poverty but would significantly increase global carbon emissions. Should they extract it?

A pharmaceutical company wants to test a new malaria vaccine in a developing country where the disease is common. The trials carry risk, but success could save millions. Is this ethical if the country lacks the regulatory infrastructure to oversee the trials?
An endangered species is destroying crops that a local community depends on for survival. The community wants to cull the animals. Conservationists object. Who is right?
Should companies be allowed to offset their carbon emissions by buying credits, or does this just give wealthy polluters permission to keep polluting?
Gene editing of human embryos should be permitted to prevent heritable disease
Gene editing for enhancement (height, intelligence) should be permanently banned
Parents should be allowed to select embryos by genetic profile via IVF
Cloning of human beings should be prohibited under international law
Gene drives should be used to eradicate malaria-carrying mosquito populations
De-extinction of species like the woolly mammoth is a worthwhile use of resources
Genetically modified crops should be labeled by law
Synthetic biology research should require a global licensing regime
A person's genome is personal property they should be able to sell
CRISPR research on human germ-line cells should be publicly funded
Direct-to-consumer DNA testing services should be banned
Designer-baby technology, once safe, should be available to anyone who can pay
Government funding for crewed Mars missions is justified given problems on Earth
Space exploration should be led by private companies, not national agencies
Asteroid mining rights should be ownable by private firms
A permanent human settlement on the Moon should be an international priority
Sending humans to space is no longer worth the cost when robots can do the science
Earth should broadcast its location to potential extraterrestrial civilizations
Planetary protection rules that limit Mars exploration are too strict
Space should be legally designated a global commons closed to military use
Billionaire-funded space tourism is an irresponsible use of resources
Humanity has a moral obligation to become a multi-planet species
Nuclear power should be central to decarbonization efforts
Governments should fund large-scale solar geoengineering research
Carbon taxes are a more effective climate policy than cap-and-trade
Wealthy nations owe climate reparations to developing countries
Individual lifestyle change is a meaningful climate strategy, not a distraction
Carbon capture technology is a dangerous excuse to delay emissions cuts
A legal framework should grant rights to rivers, forests, and ecosystems
Lab-grown meat should replace conventional livestock farming
Degrowth is a more honest climate strategy than green growth
Banning single-use plastics meaningfully reduces environmental harm
The search for a theory of everything is a worthwhile use of physics funding
Building a larger particle collider is justified given the cost
Long-term storage of nuclear waste is a solvable engineering problem
